title: Credential Compromise via Abnormal CNP-to-CP Transaction Ratio Shift
narrative: Fraudsters test stolen card credentials through small card-not-present transactions before attempting larger purchases, creating an unusual transaction mix. The compromised card shows a dramatic shift in channel usage patterns—historically a card-present heavy card suddenly exhibits elevated CNP activity. Individual transaction amounts remain within the cardholder's normal range and velocity appears acceptable, but card-level counters reveal the credential is now being used across multiple digital merchants. The C14 counter (unique merchant categories) climbs while C8 (address diversity) increases, indicating the card is touching new merchant types and shipping destinations inconsistent with prior card-present retail patterns. Device fingerprints show first-time pairings with the card despite the card having substantial transaction history.
indicators: C13 > 24 (p90), elevated card-merchant counter indicating recent cross-merchant usage surge
C14 > 7 (p90), card touching far more merchant categories than cardholder baseline
C8 > 29 (p90), card associated with many distinct billing addresses in rolling window
D1 = 0, device has no prior pairing history with this card number
card1_amt_zscore between -1.0 and +1.5, amount within normal cardholder range masking fraud
card1_distinct_addr1 > 8, card linked to numerous shipping addresses in recent period
V258 > 4.2 (engineered feature detecting CNP-to-CP ratio anomaly at card level)
indicator_explanations: Card recently used at an unusually high number of different merchants
Card suddenly appearing across many more merchant categories than historical pattern
Card associated with many different billing addresses in short timeframe
Device and card combination has never been seen together before
Transaction amount fits cardholder's typical spending pattern exactly
Card shipping to far more distinct addresses than legitimate usage supports
Vesta model detects abnormal channel-mix shift from physical to online transactions
summary: A card with established card-present history suddenly shows elevated card-not-present activity across multiple merchants and addresses, while individual transaction behavior appears normal. Device-card pairing is new despite the card having substantial prior use.
reasoning: Legitimate cardholders maintain consistent channel preferences—a card-present heavy user doesn't suddenly execute numerous CNP transactions across diverse merchants. The elevated C13, C14, and C8 counters combined with new device pairing indicate stolen credentials being monetized through digital channels while amounts stay in-pattern to avoid behavioral flags.
